ORIGINAL RESEARCH article

Front. Built Environ., 09 October 2023

Sec. Bridge Engineering

Volume 9 - 2023 | <https://doi.org/10.3389/fbuil.2023.1254269>

This article is part of the Research TopicImproving Bridge Performance Subjected to Aging and Natural Disasters[View all 4 articles](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/58096/improving-bridge-performance-subjected-to-aging-and-natural-disasters/articles)

# Machine learning approach for predicting bridge components’ condition ratings

![Md. Manik Mia](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2369808/74)
![Sabarethinam Kameshwar
](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2369797/74)

Information on bridge condition rating is critical to make decisions regarding rehabilitation or replacement of bridges. Currently, bridge components’ condition ratings are evaluated manually using inspection reports. Markov chain and Petri net models are most commonly used for predicting future values of bridge parameters, however, applicability of these models for a regional or statewide portfolio of bridges may be limited. The existing data based models have low prediction accuracy. Hence, a data and machine learning based approach is presented herein for predicting the future condition values of major components—deck, superstructure and substructure—in a portfolio of bridges with an objective to develop a more accurate approach. National Bridge Inventory (NBI) was used to get information on current and past bridge components’ condition from year 1992–2019 along with other parameters such as ownership, maintenance responsibility and age. After selecting important parameters, this data was used to train three RUSBoost based random forest models for predicting future values of deck, superstructure, and substructure conditions, respectively. The prediction accuracy of the developed models were found above 93%, thereby addressing the limitation of poor prediction accuracy of the existing studies. Additionally, the uncertainties associated with the random forest based predictions were quantified at the regional level and for individual bridges. On-system concrete pre-cast slab units and steel I-beam bridges in Louisiana were selected to demonstrate the proposed approach and predict bridge components condition ratings for years 2020 and 2021.

## 1 Introduction

Almost 40% of all bridges in the United States are at least 50 years old and 9.1% are considered as structurally deficient ([ASCE, 2017](#B4)). As a result, maintenance of these bridges has become a great challenge for federal, state and local governments. Therefore, to identify needs for maintenance, rehabilitation and replacement of bridges, transportation agencies collect data on bridge condition. Generally, all bridges located on public roads with length more than 20 feet (6.1 m) are inspected at least once in every 2 years ([Pallepogu, 2022](#B43)) and data on 116 National Bridge Inventory (NBI) items are reported annually. The National Bridge Inspection Standards (NBISs) that were implemented by Federal Highway Administration in 1968 ([Lichtenstein, 1993](#B34)) forms the basis of bridge inspection in the United States. As a part of these inspections, three major bridge components--deck, superstructure and substructure-- are assessed and rated by bridge inspectors. These NBI condition ratings are measured on a scale of 0–9 as per the NBI coding guide. These condition ratings serve as inputs for load posting decisions and resources allocation for the short and long term management of bridges.

A review of existing literature reveals that numerous studies have been conducted worldwide in predicting bridge condition ratings. Some researchers developed models to predict condition ratings for the bridge CoRe elements (deck, superstructure, substructure and culvert), and some developed models that predicted ratings of the bridges. Previous research included models using traditional statistical analysis, Markov chain, fuzzy logic, logistic regression, neural network, ensemble learning algorithm. Markov chain has been commonly used to predict future condition rating of bridge structural components ([Jiang et al., 1988](#B25); [Norris and Norris, 1998](#B42); [Morcous, 2006](#B40); [Fernando et al., 2013](#B17); [Hong et al., 2013](#B23); [Le and Andrews, 2013](#B30); [Wellalage et al., 2015](#B53); [Jamalipour et al., 2017](#B24); [Mohammed Abdelkader et al., 2019](#B39); [Goyal et al., 2020](#B21); [Srikanth and Arockiasamy, 2020](#B51)). Markov chains are state-based models where the future state of a stochastic variable is only dependent on its present state and are relatively easy to construct with fast solutions ([Le and Andrews, 2015](#B31)). However, Markov chains have some drawbacks such as: a) high quality deterioration data is needed to develop transition probability matrices and b) assumptions on constant deterioration rates that are not feasible. These drawbacks have been addressed by several methods, e.g., semi-Markov models have been used for predicting future condition of bridge components ([Kleiner, 2001](#B28); [Mishalani and Madanat, 2002](#B38); [Yang et al., 2009](#B54); [Sobanjo et al., 2010](#B50); [Mašović and Hajdin, 2014](#B37); [Fang and Sun, 2019](#B15); [Furtado and Ribeiro, 2023](#B20)). Such semi-Markov models use a Weibull distribution for modeling the residence time in different states in order to model increasing deterioration rates. However, this approach experiences an exponentially increasing number of model states as the model complexity increased ([Andrews, 2013](#B3)). More recently, [Collins (2023)](#B11) developed a framework utilizing state based Markov chain, time based Weibull and Mechanistic based models to predict future condition of bridge components. Their multiple-model approach showed good accuracy, however, only 3 years data (2008–2010) were used from the NBI database for the development of proposed deterioration modeling. Another method that addressed the drawbacks of Markov chain model is Petri nets known as PT nets which offers a flexible and efficient method to model deterioration and maintenance processes. [Le et al. (2017)](#B32) used Petri nets to model condition states of bridges with variable deterioration rates incorporating effects of bridge inspection, maintenance and correlated component performance. However, their proposed approach was limited to railway bridge only. There are some other studies available on Petri nets ([Yianni et al., 2017](#B55); [Dean et al., 2018](#B14); [Fecarotti and Andrews, 2020](#B16); [Calvert et al., 2021](#B9); [Ferreira et al., 2022](#B18)), but applicability of this method for a regional and statewide portfolio of bridges may be limited.

Numerous studies have modeled the deterioration rates of bridge components’ using machine learning. [Contreras-Nieto et al. (2018)](#B12) used logistic regression, decision trees, gradient boosting, support vector machine and neural network to predict deficiency of bridge superstructure. Logistic regression was reported to be the most effective model; however, their research was limited to steel superstructure. [Assaad and El-adaway (2020)](#B5) used artificial neural networks (ANNs) and k-nearest neighbors to evaluate and predict current bridge deck deterioration conditions. Neural network model was reported to be the most accurate with an accuracy of 91.44%. However, their model was limited to bridge deck only. Similarly, a convolutional neural network (CNN) was developed by [Liu and Zhang (2020)](#B35) to predict future condition of bridge deck, superstructure and substructure component with prediction accuracy of about 85%. [Martinez et al. (2020)](#B36) compared different classification models (k-nearest neighbors (k-NN), decision trees (DTs), linear regression (LR), artificial neural networks (ANNs), deep learning neural networks (DLNs) to predict Bridge Condition Index (BCI) for the bridges in Ontario, Canada. From their study, decision tree model was found most accurate model for the prediction of BCI. More recently, [Rajkumar et al. (2023)](#B45) developed an autoencoder-random forest model (AE-RF) to predict the condition rating of bridge components, however, the prediction accuracy were 79%, 78% and 77% for deck, superstructure and substructure condition ratings, respectively.

Researchers also used NBI data to assess current condition of bridge components’ for better management of bridges ([Kim and Yoon, 2010](#B26); [Bektas et al., 2013](#B6); [Bektaş, 2017](#B7); [Saeed et al., 2017](#B47)). [Bektas et al. (2013)](#B6) developed a new methodology to estimate NBI condition ratings from element condition data using classification and regression trees (CARTs). However, *R*2 values from their study were low (approximately 0.40) and the predicted condition rating values always remained above 5 on a scale varying from 0 to 10 ([Bektaş, 2017](#B7)). The low *R*2 values were due to the severe skewness in the NBI data as bridges with poor condition rating only constitute a tiny fraction of NBI database ([Contreras-Nieto et al., 2018](#B12)). Therefore, better models are needed to predict bridge condition rating considering this deficiency and skewness in the NBI database. [Li and Song (2022)](#B33) developed an ensemble based model to predict bridge deck defect condition. Six ensemble learning models (Random Forest, Extra Tree, AdaBoost, GBDT, XGBoost, and LightGBM) were developed and superstructure condition was reported to be the most important factor for deck defect prediction. Although they extensively investigated the use of ensemble learning methods, it was limited to bridge deck. Similarly, [Rashidi Nasab and Elzarka (2023)](#B46) also used several machine learning models to predict bridge deck deterioration and found ensemble methods, such as random forests, to have the highest accuracy. However, they did not predict the future condition of other bridge components. [Alipour et al. (2017)](#B2) used random forests to identify load posted bridges using NBI data, but they did not predict the future condition ratings of bridges.

The above discussion shows that several studies have assessed the future condition of bridge components. Some researchers used Commonly Recognized (CoRe) data and developed machine learning models, however, CoRe data are not publicly available. Hence, the available approach in existing studies have limitations to use since state level CoRe data may not be available to other state agencies. Furthermore, there are limited studies that predict the future values of condition ratings of all the three major bridge components (deck, super-structure, and sub-structure), and the prediction accuracy of these studies were low. So, there is a scope to improve the prediction accuracy. Furthermore, none of the existing studies have assessed the uncertainties associated with their predictions, which is important to make decisions, especially using machine learning models.

Therefore, the objective of this study is to develop a data and machine learning based approach to predict future values of deck, superstructure and substructure condition ratings for next year using publicly available NBI data. The reason for using publicly available data were twofold. 1) Use of publicly available data facilitates regional level application of the methodology without needing to gather additional information on bridges; thereby reducing the effort to apply the proposed model. Moreover, additional information on bridges may not always be available for all bridges in a region since agencies are not always mandated to record additional data, beyond what is reported in public datasets. 2) Use of non-public data, which may be specific to a state, can limit the applicability of the proposed approach to other states and regions. Therefore, publicly available data was used in this study. Herein, RUSBoost based random forest models were developed. RUSBoost algorithm is well suited to handle highly imbalanced data ([Blackard and Dean, 1999](#B8); [Seiffert et al., 2010](#B49); [Mounce et al., 2017](#B41); [Kinoshita et al., 2020](#B27); [Park and Mun, 2023](#B44)) which is the case of NBI database. Furthermore, herein, uncertainties associated with the prediction of the data-based models was also quantified. Herein, on-system concrete pre-cast slab units (COPCSS) and steel I-beam (CONIBM) bridges in Louisiana were selected to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed approach. COPCSS was selected as a representation of concrete slab bridges and CONIBM for steel beam bridges. In [Section 2](#h3), the methodology for data collection and preparation, feature selection and model development are presented. [Section 3](#h4) shows the application of the proposed approach with COPCSS and CONIBM on-system Louisiana bridges. In [Section 4](#h5), the prediction uncertainties at portfolio and bridge level were analyzed corresponding to years 2020 and 2021. Finally, [Section 5](#h6) presents the conclusions.

## 2 Methodology

This section used ON-system, i.e., bridges maintained by state department of transportation, COPCSS and CONIBM bridges in Louisiana to describe the proposed methodology to develop models that can predict the future values of bridge components’ condition ratings. Herein, a machine learning based approach was used for predicting future values of bridge components’. It is important to note here that data collected from National Bridge Inventory (NBI) database were highly imbalanced and skewed. In this research, the condition ratings that need to be estimated were discrete variables, i.e., poor, fair and good. Nevertheless, these discrete variables were converted to numeric values of 0, 1 and 2, respectively, and linear regression was tried but its performance, measured by accuracy, was poor since it is better suited for continuous outputs and not discrete data. So, multinomial logistic regression model was tried, which is an extension of binary logistic regression. Like logistic regression, multinomial logistic regression uses maximum likelihood estimation to maximize the overall accuracy. However, this approach does not work well if the data are highly imbalanced ([Maalouf and Siddiqi, 2014](#B56); [Zhang et al., 2022](#B58)) because the resulting models will tend to be biased to the majority class. Regular random forest model was also tried; however, the testing accuracy was not good enough due to the high skewness in data. Previous researchers ([Blackard and Dean, 1999](#B8); [Seiffert et al. 2008](#B48); [Seiffert et al., 2010](#B49); [Mounce et al., 2017](#B41); [Kinoshita et al., 2020](#B27); [Malek et al., 2022](#B57); [Park and Mun, 2023](#B44)) have mentioned the effectiveness of RUSBoost based random forest models to handle highly imbalanced data. RUSBoost uses a combination of RUS (random under sampling) and the standard boosting procedure AdaBoost in order to better model the minority class by under sampling the majority class data points. Hence, three RUSBoost based separate random forest models were developed to predict the future values of deck, superstructure and substructure condition ratings using bridge data from the National Bridge Inventory. The following explains the steps involved in the development of RUSBoost based random forest models and quantification of the uncertainties associated with the predictions.

### 2.1 Data preparation

[Figure 1](#F1) illustrates the steps for data preparation followed in this study. Data on bridges in Louisiana were collected from the NBI database from years 1992–2019 resulting in 303723 bridges records, i.e., data points. Since “ON” system bridges maintained by Louisiana department of transportation and development (LADOTD) were considered in this study, preliminary filtering (first diamond box in [Figure 1](#F1)) resulted in 196004 “ON” service bridge data points. Currently, visual inspection technique is most commonly used for the condition rating of bridges in United States where bridge inspectors gather a large amount of information and define the condition rating of bridge elements. Thus, bridge data is prone to numerous human errors. For instance, some bridges were found missing and not consistent through 1992 to 2019. As a result, only bridges that had data for all the years from 1992 to 2019 were used for the RUSBoost based random forest development. Any bridges that were missing in any year from 1992 to 2019 were discarded. Also, during pre-processing, it was found that some bridges did not have any records on bridge components condition ratings. Hence, bridges that had missing data in the bridge components’ condition ratings and year of construction were discarded as well. After these pre-processing steps, concrete pre-cast slab units (COPCSS) and steel I-beam (CONIBM) bridges in Louisiana were selected to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed approach. So, the filtering (second diamond box 2 in [Figure 1](#F1)) resulted in 20597 COPCSS and 4460 CONIBM bridge data records/points. Herein, a data point represents a bridge in a particular year. National Bridge Inventory (NBI) consists 116 items, however, some items such as bridge name, bridge identification number, traffic safety features, etc. have no relevance to condition ratings. Thus, these parameters were discarded initially based on judgements. After that, feature selection was carried out and significant parameters were selected which can be found in [Section 2.2](#h3-2). Data on the remaining relevant parameters collected from NBI were further processed/filtered, if necessary, for use in the machine learning based approach (refer to “Filtering needed?” box of [Figure 1](#F1)). For instance, NBI condition ratings for deck, superstructure and substructure were rated on a scale of 0–9 according to the specifications in the Recording and Coding Guide ([FHWA, 1995](#B19)). For analysis, these ratings were recategorized as poor (<5), fair (=5) and good (>5) following the recommendation of the AASHTO Manual for Bridge Inspection ([AASHTO, 2018](#B1)). Functional classifications (NBI item 26) categorizes bridges as rural or urban based on the location of roadway and it was labelled as arterial road, collector road, or a local road. Kind of Highway was not originally in NBI and defined in this study using Functional classification (Item 26) and categorized as Roads, State and Interstate. Maintenance responsibility (NBI item 21), which originally consisted of 29 categories, was recategorized as federal, state, local and other. Similarly, design loads were labelled as heavy, light and other. Scour critical ratings (NBI Item 113) were labelled from 0 to 9 based on severity. In case of age, 2019 NBI data set was used as a starting point for the analysis. So, age of a bridge was calculated as Age=2019−year built. If any bridge was reconstructed then Age=2019−year reconstructed was used to determine the age. Average daily traffic (ADT) and average daily truck traffic (ADTT) were labelled as per the study of [Hearn (2014)](#B22) shown in [Table 1](#T1).

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-g001.jpg)

**FIGURE 1**. Approach for preparing dataset.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-t001.jpg)

**TABLE 1**. Recategorized ADT and ADTT values ([Hearn, 2014](#B22)).

In addition, log (ADT) and sine of skew angle were created as these gave better performance than the original attributes ([Alipour et al., 2017](#B2)). Also, to represent the climate at a bridge’s location, its “climatic zone” was added as another attribute based on NOAA’s climatic divisions in Louisiana. Thus, data from 1992–2019 were collected to use in development of RUSBoost based random forest models.

Finally, to facilitate the use of machine learning techniques, categorical variables such as functional classification, kind of highway, maintenance responsibility, base highway network, historical significance, bridge median, structure flared, service type—42A, traffic direction, scour and climate zone were replaced using dummy coding ([Daly et al., 2016](#B13)).

### 2.2 Feature selection

Feature selection was a significant step in this study since the prepared dataset from NBI has many variables and features. Selecting most significant features would reduce the overall complexity of the final model (refer to “Important feature selection” in [Figure 2](#F2)). Random forest has become a useful feature selection algorithm that can handle datasets with a large number of variables ([Chen et al., 2020](#B10)) and was used herein. A random forest is a machine learning technique that utilizes ensemble learning where many classifiers are combined to provide solutions to a complex problem. A random forest algorithm consists of many decision trees and a prediction is obtained by taking the average or mean of the output from various trees. It relies on various decision tress and every decision tree consists of decision nodes, leaf nodes and a root node. To train a decision tree in random forest, first, a root node is created containing all training data points. The root node is then split in to two children nodes based on one of the input variables (bridge parameters such as condition ratings, geometry, age, etc.) and a corresponding threshold value. All data points which are less than the split variable’s value will be in left child node while in right child node, the split variable’s value will be larger than the threshold value. The threshold and split variable are selected based on maximum reduction in cost function. In next step, each of the child nodes are considered as root nodes and splits are further continued. This process continues until the nodes can no longer be split because of the restrictions on minimum node size (minimum data points in the node) or tree depth (number of splits) or the split does not lead to a significant reduction in the cost function. Feature importance is determined by combining the decrease in cost when a node is split using a feature and the probability of reaching that node. Higher reduction in cost or higher probability of reaching the node signifies higher importance of the feature. These importance values from each decision trees are aggregated to get feature importance value.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-g002.jpg)

**FIGURE 2**. Flowchart for random forest model development.

Following these steps, important features were selected and are shown in [Table 2](#T2). In addition to these parameters, inventory and operating rating were also found as important features but were not included herein since the values of these parameters in the future cannot be ascertained preventing machine learning models from predicting longer term future condition ratings. The data on the features in [Table 2](#T2) were collected for years 1992–2019 from NBI database. During data preparation, the component condition rating (e.g., for deck) that will be predicted was labelled as output and corresponding data from the preceding year was considered to be the input. This process was followed for years 1992–2019. Similar procedure was carried out for superstructure and substructure condition rating prediction. Details of model development are discussed in [Section 2.3](#h3-3).

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-t002.jpg)

**TABLE 2**. Important features from NBI database.

### 2.3 Model development and accuracy

As mentioned earlier, due to the large number of bridges, a data based approach was selected in this study to predict future values of bridge components’ condition ratings. Herein, three RUSBoost based random forest models were developed separately to predict future values of deck, superstructure and substructure condition ratings. These models were trained using the dataset discussed in [Section 2.1](#h3-1). 85% of total data was randomly chosen to train the model and remaining 15% data was used to test the model (“Training Set” and “Test Set” in [Figure 2](#F2)). The required parameters in a random forest model such as learning cycles, type of weak learners, weights, learning rate and cost (refer to “Random forest hyperparameter selection” in [Figure 2](#F2)) were selected using trial and error to ensure a balanced confusion matrix ([Kohavi and Provost, 1998](#B29)) with high accuracy and shown in [Table 3](#T3). The diamond box in [Figure 2](#F2) represents that unless good accuracy and balanced confusion matrix were achieved, the hyper parameters were varied to get an expected model. In [Table 3](#T3), cost matrix is a square matrix, where costi,j is the cost (penalty) associated with classifying a point into class *j* if its true class is *i*. In the cost matrix, the rows correspond to the true class and the columns correspond to the predicted class. Higher costs were used to ensure that the model did not overestimate the component conditions, i.e., originally rated as poor but predicted by model as fair or good. In addition to misclassification cost, for CONIBM category, weight of 200 was used to get good accuracy and balanced confusion matrix. Herein, weight signifies that more penalties were assigned if the model overestimated a bridge’s condition rating. For instance, if the original rating of a bridge’s deck component is poor but the model predicted as fair or good, more penalties were assigned by using weight of 200, irrespective of the predicted class.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-t003.jpg)

**TABLE 3**. Hyper-parameters used in random forest models.

A confusion matrix is a tabular summary showing the number of correct and incorrect predictions by a classifier. It is important to note that in multiclass classification problems, accuracy might not be sufficient to identify the best predictive model as a model with high accuracy could have imbalanced predictions. A good predictive model should have a balanced confusion matrix with minimal off–diagonal values since these off–diagonal values represent false positive or false negative predictions. [Table 4](#T4) shows a confusion matrix obtained in this study for deck condition rating prediction for COPCSS bridges.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-t004.jpg)

**TABLE 4**. Typical confusion matrix (Deck condition rating—COPCSS).

Furthermore, in case of imbalanced datasets, as is the case in this study, the model could be biased to the class with the greatest number of observations. As a result, the model can be considered to be acceptable if a balanced confusion matrix with high accuracy is achieved. The use of a confusion matrix as an evaluation criteria has also been suggested for highly imbalanced datasets ([Suresh, 2021](#B52)). As can be seen from [Table 4](#T4), the confusion matrix is balanced because the developed random forest model overestimated the condition for 25 bridges while underestimated it for 29 bridges. Overestimation implies that the bridge was actually poor but was predicted as fair/good by the model and *vice versa* for underestimation. Herein, 15% test data was used to check the model’s accuracy and develop the confusion matrix.

As mentioned earlier that the data collected from NBI database were highly imbalanced. For instance, while preparing the random forest model for estimating deck condition rating for COPCSS bridges, about 2.14% and 2.98% of bridges were rated to be in poor and fair condition, respectively. On the other hand, about 94.89% of bridges were rated to be in good condition. Due to this imbalance in the data, RUSBoost based random forest algorithm was used in this study to handle high imbalance in data. [Figure 2](#F2) shows the flowchart of the process used in this study to develop the random forest models to predict future values of condition ratings of deck, superstructure and substructure.

[Table 5](#T5) shows the prediction accuracy for the selected on-system concrete pre-cast slab units (COPCSS) and steel I-beam (CONIBM) bridges in Louisiana. The random forest models were highly accurate and there was small difference between the testing and training accuracy, which highlighted that the random forest models were able to learn the trends that influence the following year’s condition ratings. However, for substructure condition rating in COPCSS bridges, the training accuracy was similar to that of other models, but its testing accuracy was relatively lower. Nevertheless, the model was able to predict the following year’s sub-structure condition ratings well. Lower test accuracy for sub-structure condition rating prediction model can be attributed to higher levels of uncertainty in the condition ratings because several parts of the sub-structure are not easily accessible during bridge inspections. Similar observation was observed for deck components in case of CONIBM. However, all three models were found to predict future values of components’ condition ratings reasonably well. Hence, it can be inferred that the developed models were able to predict future condition rating values of individual bridges accurately.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1254269/fbuil-09-1254269-HTML/image_m/fbuil-09-1254269-t005.jpg)

**TABLE 5**. Prediction accuracy for COPCSS and CONIBM bridges.

What challenges are associated with integrating AI into traditional bridge monitoring systems?Integration challenges include compatibility with legacy systems, inconsistent data formats, high costs of retrofitting infrastructure, and the need for standardized data collection and processing. What are the future trends in AI-driven bridge monitoring?Future trends include advanced sensors (e.g., nano-sensors), smart materials like self-healing concrete, integration with autonomous systems (drones, robots), blockchain for secure data management, and widespread adoption of predictive maintenance systems.

How AI is Disrupting Bridge Monitoring and Maintenance?





Bridges connect regions, facilitate commerce, and ensure safe travel for millions of people. Today, there are over 600,000 bridges in the United States alone, and the numbers are comparable worldwide. The structural integrity of these bridges directly impacts public safety, economic growth, and the longevity of infrastructure. Therefore, continuous bridge monitoring is essential for ensuring their safety, performance, and durability.

Traditional methods of bridge inspection, such as visual assessments and manual load testing, have long been the go-to approaches. However, there are certain limitations to these methods. They can be subjective, prone to human error, and labor-intensive. Also, they typically provide limited insight into the hidden structural issues that could lead to costly repairs or, in the worst case, catastrophic failures. For example, a study by the Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) noted that around 40% of U.S. bridges are over 50 years old, many of which are deteriorating faster than they can be inspected or repaired.

As these infrastructures age, it becomes increasingly crucial to incorporate more technologies to monitor and assess their health effectively. The need for efficient, precise, and proactive solutions is paramount, and this is where Artificial Intelligence (AI) comes in.

Read more: How will AI-powered Machines enhance Construction and Building Design?



1. AI’s Role in Infrastructure: How Artificial Intelligence is Transforming Bridge Monitoring

Artificial Intelligence has disrupted many industries, and infrastructure is no exception. AI’s integration into bridge monitoring introduces a level of precision, scalability, and automation that traditional methods simply cannot achieve. Using AI, engineers can now detect anomalies, predict failures, and assess structural health in real time—greatly improving decision-making processes.

Machine learning (ML) algorithms can analyze vast amounts of sensor data to identify patterns and predict potential structural failures before they occur. Predictive analytics enables the shift from reactive to proactive maintenance, helping infrastructure owners and engineers prevent catastrophic issues such as bridge collapse. AI-powered tools and devices, such as smart sensors, IoT devices, and edge computing, collect and analyze real-time data from a bridge’s environment and structure. The ability to process and analyze this data immediately allows for early detection of even minor problems, reducing repair costs and increasing the lifespan of these critical assets.

Incorporating AI also enables the development of digital twins—virtual models of bridges that simulate near real-time conditions, allowing engineers to test different scenarios and foresee future issues based on historical and real-time data. These capabilities, combined with predictive maintenance systems, enhance the efficiency, safety, and lifespan of bridges while lowering overall costs.



2. Challenges in Bridge Monitoring

Limitations of Traditional Methods

For decades, bridge monitoring has largely relied on manual inspections and visual assessments. While these methods are foundational, they are fraught with limitations that impact their effectiveness and reliability. Manual inspections require engineers or inspectors to visually assess the bridge’s structural components, often focusing on visible damage such as cracks, corrosion, or deterioration of the surface. However, the limitations of these assessments are evident.

This underscores the need for more efficient, data-driven approaches that offer continuous monitoring and deeper insights into the structural health of bridges.



Need for Proactive Maintenance

One of the most significant challenges with traditional bridge monitoring is the reactive nature of maintenance. Typically, structural issues are addressed only after they become noticeable or have already reached a level requiring expensive repairs and downtime. This approach is not only costly but also risky, as it could result in critical issues going unnoticed or being addressed too late.

AI technologies, such as predictive analytics, machine learning (ML) models, and near real-time monitoring systems, are changing the paradigm of maintenance from a reactive approach to a proactive one. With the help of smart sensors and IoT devices, AI can continuously monitor and analyze the health of a bridge by measuring various parameters, such as strain, displacement, vibration, and temperature.

Read more: Technological Advancements in Structural Health Monitoring (SHM): AI, ML, IoT, and Sensor Innovations



3. AI Technologies for Bridge Monitoring

Data Collection & Analysis: AI-Driven Sensors, IoT Devices, and Real-Time Systems Gathering Structural Data

One of the primary factors driving the shift to AI in bridge monitoring is the ability to gather vast amounts of data through geotechnical sensors and Internet of Things (IoT) devices. These technologies provide a more comprehensive view of a bridge's structural health than traditional methods could ever achieve..

AI-driven data collection systems offer a data-rich environment for engineers to understand the behavior of structures in their daily operations. This continuous flow of high-quality, near-real-time data is invaluable for ensuring bridges remain safe and functional over the long term.



Predictive Analytics: Machine Learning Models Identifying Patterns and Anomalies for Early Detection

Predictive analytics is one of the key components of AI that has transformed how we monitor and maintain bridges. Machine learning models are capable of analyzing the vast amounts of data collected by sensors and IoT devices to identify patterns and predict future behaviors of the bridge’s structure. This predictive power helps engineers detect potential issues before they become critical, reducing the need for emergency repairs and ensuring safety.

By utilizing predictive analytics, AI transforms the way bridges are maintained, shifting the focus from reactive repairs to proactive, data-driven interventions that can significantly reduce the risks and costs associated with infrastructure management.

Read more: Geohub: Empowering Minds in Geotechnical and Infrastructure Engineering



Digital Twins: Virtual Replicas of Bridges Enabling Real-Time Monitoring and Simulation

Digital twins is a virtual model that allows engineers to observe the performance and condition of a bridge in a digital space, simulating different conditions and scenarios to predict how the structure will behave over time.

Engineers can use digital twins to simulate extreme conditions, such as high traffic loads, seismic events, or environmental stressors like heavy rain or snow. By testing these scenarios in a virtual environment, they can assess how the bridge will behave under these stresses and plan for potential maintenance needs or improvements.

They can be used to test changes to the structure before making modifications, allowing for better-informed decisions. Additionally, by continuously monitoring the digital twin, engineers can observe the effects of repairs or upgrades, ensuring the modifications are effective.

The integration of digital twins with AI creates a powerful tool for bridge monitoring and management, enabling real-time, predictive insights into the bridge’s performance and lifecycle.



Smart Sensors & Edge Computing

While collecting vast amounts of data is crucial for effective bridge monitoring, the ability to process and analyze that data quickly is equally important. Edge computing plays a significant role in this process by enabling localized data processing near the source of data collection.

By leveraging smart sensors and edge computing, AI-driven bridge monitoring systems provide instantaneous insights and allow for immediate intervention, ensuring the bridge remains safe and fully functional.



4. Integration Challenges

Compatibility Concerns: Issues Integrating AI with Legacy Systems and Traditional Monitoring Methods

Traditional monitoring methods often rely on hardware and software that were not designed for modern AI applications. Many existing monitoring systems are standalone, manual, or based on outdated technology, which may not be compatible with newer AI systems that require sophisticated sensors, near real-time data analytics, and IoT connectivity. Integrating AI tools with these older systems may involve complex retrofitting of infrastructure or a complete overhaul of monitoring processes.

AI-driven bridge monitoring relies heavily on big data—collected through IoT devices, sensors, and real-time monitoring systems. For AI models to operate effectively, the data must be collected, stored, and processed in a standardized manner. Integrating AI technologies with existing systems that collect data in non-standard formats can be a challenge. Inconsistent data formats, gaps in data, or siloed data across different departments or locations can make it difficult to get a comprehensive view of a bridge’s health and performance.

The cost of integrating AI with legacy systems can be a barrier for many infrastructure owners, especially those with limited budgets. Retrofitting old monitoring systems, upgrading sensors, installing IoT devices, and implementing the necessary AI-driven software can involve substantial investments. Smaller contractors or municipalities may find it financially unfeasible to implement these technologies without clear cost-benefit analysis and long-term planning.

To overcome these challenges, it is crucial to adopt a gradual, phased approach to integration, ensuring that new technologies are compatible with existing systems without causing disruption. AI integration should be seen as an ongoing evolution, where older systems are incrementally enhanced to incorporate new capabilities.

To resolve data integration challenges, it is important to choose AI platforms and monitoring systems that are interoperable with existing infrastructure. Proqio- the data intelligence software for infrastructure is designed to work seamlessly with a variety of data sources, using standardized communication protocols and open-source frameworks. This makes it easier to integrate newer technologies with legacy systems, reducing the complexity of data processing and improving accessibility.

Read more: Civil Construction Design: Definition, Importance, Key Aspects

5. Cost-Benefit Analysis

The cost of implementing AI for bridge monitoring includes the installation of smart sensors, IoT devices, real-time data collection systems, and the software required to analyze and interpret the data. Also, there may be costs associated with training staff to operate these advanced technologies and integrating AI with existing legacy systems. Depending on the scope of the implementation, initial costs can range from thousands to millions of dollars.

Despite the significant upfront investment, the long-term savings and operational efficiencies derived from AI-driven monitoring are substantial. AI enables early detection of structural issues, which helps avoid expensive emergency repairs, downtime, and catastrophic failures. For instance, predictive maintenance allows for interventions before major issues occur, reducing the need for costly and disruptive repairs that could otherwise impact bridge performance.

Furthermore, AI-driven monitoring systems can optimize maintenance schedules, ensuring that resources are allocated efficiently. This results in fewer routine inspections and lower costs for labor and materials. The ability to predict the remaining lifespan of critical components also allows for better budgeting and resource allocation, maximizing the return on investment (ROI) over time.

As more infrastructure assets (such as bridges, tunnels, and highways) are integrated into AI monitoring systems, the cost per unit for data collection, processing, and analysis decreases. This economy of scale allows infrastructure owners to achieve greater coverage with a lower overall investment, making the technology more affordable as it scales.



6. Future Trends

AI Advancements: Upcoming Technologies Enhancing Bridge Monitoring Capabilities

As AI technologies continue to evolve, the future of bridge monitoring looks even more promising. As more data is collected and AI systems become smarter, the next generation of machine learning algorithms will be able to identify even subtler patterns and anomalies in bridge behavior. These algorithms will become more adept at predicting failure points with greater accuracy, helping engineers identify risks that were previously undetectable. New models will also improve the interpretation of complex data sets, making it easier to derive actionable insights from large quantities of information.

Future bridge monitoring systems will likely incorporate even more advanced sensors, including nano-sensors, which can be embedded directly into materials during construction. These sensors will be capable of continuously monitoring the internal structure of a bridge for signs of stress, corrosion, or other types of degradation. Smart materials, such as self-healing concrete, will further enhance the durability and longevity of bridges. These materials may be able to respond to environmental conditions or structural stress in real time, providing invaluable data to AI systems for better predictive maintenance.

AI-based bridge monitoring systems may integrate more deeply with autonomous systems, such as drones and robotic inspection tools. Drones equipped with advanced sensors could be deployed to inspect hard-to-reach areas of a bridge, providing real-time data to AI systems. Similarly, robots may be used to carry out minor repairs or maintenance tasks autonomously, reducing the need for human intervention and ensuring that problems are addressed quickly and efficiently.

With the rise of IoT devices and connected sensors, ensuring the integrity of the collected data becomes more important than ever. Blockchain technology could play a key role in this by providing a secure, tamper-proof ledger of all data collected from sensors and AI systems. This would not only improve the transparency of monitoring but also help mitigate issues related to data manipulation or errors, ensuring the accuracy and reliability of all reports and insights generated.

The future of bridge monitoring also hinges on the widespread adoption of AI technologies across the globe. As these systems become more affordable and accessible, governments, infrastructure owners, and contractors will increasingly adopt AI-based monitoring tools, leading to widespread improvements in public safety and resource management.

Read more: Artificial Intelligence in Civil Infrastructure Health Monitoring

Policymakers will benefit from AI technologies by gaining access to more accurate, up-to-date information about the condition of bridges and other infrastructure. With this data, governments can make better-informed decisions about resource allocation, maintenance schedules, and safety regulations. AI will also allow for the development of predictive models to guide long-term infrastructure planning and investment strategies, ensuring that critical projects are prioritized based on actual needs rather than guesswork.

As AI technologies continue to advance and become more widely adopted, the impact on public safety, infrastructure management, and resource allocation will be profound. The future of bridge monitoring will be shaped by data-driven decision-making, enabling smarter and more efficient management of infrastructure assets.



FAQs

1. Why is bridge monitoring important for public safety and infrastructure longevity?Bridge monitoring ensures structural integrity, which directly impacts public safety, economic growth, and infrastructure durability. Regular monitoring helps identify potential issues before they become critical, thereby preventing accidents and costly repairs.

2. What are the limitations of traditional bridge monitoring methods?Traditional methods like visual inspections and manual load testing can be subjective, prone to human error, labor-intensive, and limited to surface-level assessments. They often fail to detect underlying structural issues and are reactive rather than proactive.

3. How does AI enhance bridge monitoring compared to traditional methods?AI offers precision, scalability, and automation by analyzing large volumes of sensor data in real time. It helps detect anomalies, predict failures, and monitor structural health proactively, reducing maintenance costs and enhancing safety.

4. What role do machine learning algorithms play in bridge monitoring?Machine learning algorithms analyze sensor data to identify patterns and predict potential structural failures before they occur. This allows engineers to take preventive measures, reducing repair costs and improving bridge lifespan.

5. What are digital twins, and how do they contribute to bridge monitoring?Digital twins are virtual models of bridges that simulate real-time conditions, allowing engineers to test scenarios, predict issues, and monitor repairs' effectiveness. They enhance predictive maintenance and decision-making capabilities.

6. How do smart sensors and IoT devices contribute to bridge monitoring?Smart sensors and IoT devices collect real-time data on strain, displacement, vibration, temperature, and other parameters. They provide a continuous flow of data, which AI systems analyze for early detection of structural issues.

7. What challenges are associated with integrating AI into traditional bridge monitoring systems?Integration challenges include compatibility with legacy systems, inconsistent data formats, high costs of retrofitting infrastructure, and the need for standardized data collection and processing.

8. What are the cost benefits of using AI-driven bridge monitoring systems?While AI integration requires significant upfront investment, long-term savings are achieved through early detection of issues, optimized maintenance schedules, reduced downtime, and lower costs for labor and materials.

9. How does edge computing enhance AI-driven bridge monitoring systems?Edge computing allows localized data processing near the data source, ensuring faster response times, reducing data transfer requirements, and enabling immediate intervention for critical issues.

10. What are the future trends in AI-driven bridge monitoring?Future trends include advanced sensors (e.g., nano-sensors), smart materials like self-healing concrete, integration with autonomous systems (drones, robots), blockchain for secure data management, and widespread adoption of predictive maintenance systems.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Predictive analytics employs machine learning algorithms to analyze historical bridge data, enabling accurate forecasts of structural health and maintenance needs, thereby facilitating proactive interventions.
Machine learning excels in anomaly detection by identifying deviations from established patterns in sensor data, allowing for early warning signs of potential structural failures in bridges.

Machine learning models demonstrate enhanced predictive accuracy compared to traditional analysis methods, utilizing vast datasets to minimize errors and optimize bridge maintenance strategies effectively.
